title: The Return Test Every New Piece of Debt Should Pass Before You Sign
linkedin:
A bank says yes to your loan. That is not the same as the loan being a good idea.

Approval only measures one thing: can the business service the repayments from the cash it already generates. It says nothing about whether the machine, the second site or the extra hire it is funding will actually return more than it costs.

We see this gap most clearly right now. FY26 has closed, the new financial year has some momentum, and the growth decisions parked over EOFY are moving again. Almost every one of those conversations starts with "will the repayments fit" and almost never with "what does this need to earn back."

Every piece of debt carries a hurdle. At minimum, it is the all-in cost of the debt itself, the rate plus fees plus any security cost. But the real hurdle sits higher than that, because even a loan that merely breaks even against its own interest rate still consumes management time and adds a fixed repayment the business now carries regardless of how the investment performs.

The businesses that borrow well are rarely the ones with the lowest rate on offer. They are the ones who worked out what the investment needed to return before they walked into the meeting, so approval and return never got confused with each other.

Full piece on the ProfitPulse blog.

#FractionalCFO #SMEFinance #FinancialStrategy
facebook:
Quick question for the business owners here: when you last took out finance for equipment, a hire or a new site, did you work out what it needed to earn back, or just whether you could afford the repayments?

Most owners run the second test. The bank already ran that one for you, in its own interest. The first test, whether the thing you are funding actually returns more than it costs, is the one that tends to get skipped, especially when a decision feels obviously right at the time.

Genuinely curious how many of you have a number in mind before you sign, versus finding out afterwards.

#SMEFinance
google:
A note for local business owners as growth decisions start moving again after EOFY. Getting approved for finance, whether it is equipment, a vehicle, or a loan for a second location, only tells you the repayments fit the business's current cash flow. It does not tell you whether the investment itself will earn back more than the loan costs.

We see this pattern often with owner-led businesses across Queensland, New South Wales and Victoria. A decision gets judged on affordability alone, and the question of what return it actually needs to generate never gets written down. That is a different test, and it is the one worth running before signing rather than after.

A simple rule helps: take the true cost of the finance, interest plus fees plus any security cost, add a margin for the management time and risk involved, and that becomes the return the investment has to clear. A second site, a new hire or a piece of equipment can then be judged against that number rather than against how the decision feels in the moment.

If you are weighing up a growth decision at the moment and haven't run that number yet, it's worth doing before the next lender conversation. Read the full piece on the ProfitPulse blog.
